metadata.title: Cost-Performance Friction (B4)
metadata.description: The frontier model is not always the most-used model. As capability rises, so does inference cost. Most production agents route to a cheaper 'good-enough' model, which chokes the data flywheel for the leader.
metadata.author: Tracking AI Course

nodes.label: Model Capability
nodes.description: Aggregate quality of the frontier model.

nodes.label: Inference Cost
nodes.description: Per-token cost to run the frontier model in production.

nodes.label: User Adoption
nodes.description: Number of active users, agents, and enterprise integrations running on the frontier model.

nodes.label: Feedback Data
nodes.description: Volume and richness of real-world training signal feeding back into the model.

edges.description: Bigger, better frontier models cost more per token to serve.
Higher inference cost suppresses adoption — production agents route to cheaper 'good-enough' models.
More usage produces more feedback data.
Feedback data improves the model — which is exactly what gets choked off when adoption falls.

loops.label: B4: Cost-Performance Friction
loops.description: The cheapest competent model often beats the smartest expensive one in market terms — a real-world counterweight to the runaway hypothesis.
loops.behavior_pattern: Self-limiting capability growth through unit economics.
